# `UDP S->C 0x03/0x28` — Reliable/WorldInfo

**Transport:** UDP  
**Direction:** S->C  
**Identifier:** `0x03/0x28`  
**Status:** verified

## Structure

Per-NPC zone-population broadcast. Each zone-resident NPC is
emitted via a separate {@code 0x03/0x28} carrying its ID,
position, ASCII type-name, and orientation. Verified 2026-05-09
against 83 samples drawn from 6 retail captures (AUGUSTO, CASH,
CREATION, DRSTONE, DRSTONE3, DRSTONE4).

**Common body layout (post {@code 0x28} sub-op):**

```
[0]      0x28                    sub-opcode (constant)
[1..2]   0x0001 LE16             CONSTANT prefix (83/83 samples)
[3..4]   entity_id LE16          per-NPC unique within zone
[5..6]   0x0000                  reserved padding
[7..10]  LE32 entity hash / UID  varies per NPC (likely instance hash)
[11..12] entity_class LE16       e.g. 0x014f (varies — possibly NPC
                                  template / model id)
[13..14] Y_raw LE16              quantized world-Y (with offset)
[15..16] Z_raw LE16              quantized world-Z
[17..18] X_raw LE16              quantized world-X
[19..23] 5-byte status block     animation/orientation/state
[24..33] 10-byte attribute slots mostly zeros (NPC stats array?)
[34..N]  ASCII type name         NULL-terminated string
[N+1..end] ASCII orientation int NULL-terminated decimal number
                                  (e.g. "1", "290", "-10", "180")
```

### Sample decode (AUGUSTO #1, 43 B):

```
28 00 01 24 01 00 00 6a 51 9a 37 4f 01 c8 77 00
7e 7f 7c 00 ca 06 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00
00 00 50 4d 41 4e 00 31 00
```

- `[0]` 0x28
- `[1..2]` 00 01 — constant
- `[3..4]` 24 01 — entity_id = 0x0124 (292)
- `[5..6]` 00 00 — padding
- `[7..10]` 6a 51 9a 37 — entity hash 0x379a516a
- `[11..12]` 4f 01 — entity_class = 0x014f
- `[13..14]` c8 77 — Y_raw (=30664)
- `[15..16]` 00 7e — Z_raw (=32256)
- `[17..18]` 7f 7c — X_raw (=31871)
- `[19..23]` ca 06 00 00 00 — status (5B)
- `[24..33]` 10 zeros — empty attribute slots
- `[34..]` 50 4d 41 4e 00 — ASCII "PMAN\0"
- `[..end]` 31 00 — ASCII "1\0" (orientation/level)

## Variants

Body sizes 42–53 B (avg 43 B) — variation comes entirely from the
trailing ASCII strings (NPC type-name + orientation int are
variable-length).

ASCII type names observed across the 83-sample subset:
- `WSK` — most common (multiple captures, varied orientations
  60/100/180/15/90/-90)
- `WDK` — CREATION (90, -90)
- `PMAN` — AUGUSTO (1)
- `SSCORPA` — AUGUSTO (0) — probably "SSCORPION" or similar
- `STATIC` — DRSTONE/DRSTONE3 (-10, 120) — possibly inanimate
  objects (props, doors)
- `BUMP1` — DRSTONE4 (180)
- `WTNOWEAPO` — CASH (270) — truncated "WTNOWEAPON" or similar
- (catalog mentions more — partial 4-letter codes that align to
  NC2 NPC type table)

These match the NC2 client's NPC template table (one ASCII name
per NPC class). Ceres-J would need the same mapping to emit
byte-correct WorldInfo per NPC.

## Observed contexts

Top markers (within ±2s of emission):
- `IN_WORLD × 33` — fires constantly during normal gameplay
- `AFTER_CHAR_SELECT_WORLDENTRY × 21` — bursts at zone entry
- `AFTER_ENTER_SEWER × 2` — emits when entering a sub-zone
- `IN_SUBWAY_CAR × 1` — emits in transit zones

Per-capture concentration: RETAIL_VEHICLE_DRONE × 1,489 (45% of
all obs) — combat captures emit heavy NPC-population data. All
17/17 captures have it (ubiquitous).

## Open questions

- Byte 11..12 entity_class LE16: maps to which NC2 client table?
  Looks like the model/skeleton ID, not the same space as raw
  0x1b's entity_class_id at [13..14] (which is mostly 0x88b9 in
  HANNIBAL).
- Byte 19..23 5-byte status block: layout? Some samples have
  `06 05 03 04 04 02` shape, others differ — looks like a
  6-element u8 array (NPC vital stats? aggro level? AI state?).
- Byte 24..33 10-byte attribute slots: mostly zero in samples.
  When non-zero (CASH/DRSTONE samples), what do they encode?
- ASCII "WTNOWEAPO" — truncated? Or padded with NULs? Catalog
  may show longer variants.

## Task #178 — NPC-render byte-diff (2026-05-16)

Byte-diffed the verified AUGUSTO "PMAN" decode (entity 0x0124,
41 B inner incl. 0x28) and the three preserved raw samples
(`docs/protocol/_data/packets.json`, entities 0x0149/0x013e/0x0130)
against what `WorldNPCInfo` / `ZoneStateCompoundPacket` Datagram 3
emitted. The raw retail pcaps are not present in this checkout; the
catalog keeps only 3 truncated (32-B) samples per type plus this
hand-decoded reference, so a 331-packet diff is **not reproducible**
here — an earlier WIP comment claiming one was discredited and
removed.

| doc off | retail (PMAN / samples)        | pre-#178 Ceres        | verdict |
|---------|--------------------------------|-----------------------|---------|
| [0]     | `28`                           | `28`                  | OK |
| [1..2]  | `00 01`                        | `00 01`               | OK |
| [3..4]  | entity_id LE16                 | `mapID` LE16          | OK |
| [5..6]  | `00 00`                        | `00 00`               | OK |
| [7..10] | per-NPC handle (all distinct: `6a519a37`, `93efed78`, `27ebed78`, `761dee78`) | **constant `8958887`** | **BUG → fixed** (deterministic per-NPC handle) |
| [11..12]| class_id (PMAN `4f 01`)        | `getType()` LE16      | OK |
| [13..14]| Y LE16                         | Y LE16                | OK |
| [15..16]| Z LE16                         | Z LE16                | OK |
| [17..18]| X LE16                         | X LE16                | OK |
| [19..33]| **15-byte** state block (PMAN `00 ca 06` + 12×00) | **16 B**: spurious `0x22` "zone area" + filler | **BUG → fixed** (15 zero bytes; strings were 1 B late) |
| [34..]  | type_name`\0` orientation`\0`  | scriptName`\0` modelName`\0` | **BUG → fixed** (name + ASCII angle) |

Confirmed protocol bugs (retail-evidenced) fixed in `WorldNPCInfo`:
1. `[7..10]` was a hard-coded constant `8958887` for **every** NPC;
   retail proves it is per-NPC and that constant appears in **none**
   of the available evidence. The client keys its world-object table
   on this handle, so every NPC collided onto one reference.
2. The middle block was 16 B (incl. a fabricated `0x22`), pushing
   the trailing strings one byte past where retail puts them; retail
   has exactly **15** bytes there.
3. Trailing strings were `scriptName\0 modelName\0`; retail is
   `type_name\0 orientation\0`.

These are genuine wire-protocol errors backed by the retail decode,
fixed to match exactly. The `[19..33]` block's *internal*
sub-structure (PMAN's live `00 ca 06 …`) is per-NPC runtime state
and an open question; we emit the retail-valid all-zero "no-state"
instance rather than fabricate it.

### Raw `0x1b` `[13..14]` entity_class_id — RESOLVED (2026-05-17)

The earlier note here said this was unfixable without a retail
capture. That was wrong on both counts. The retail captures **are**
in the tree (`ceres-j/strace/*.pcap`) and decoding AUGUSTO/NORMAN/
DRSTONE4 with `tools/pcap-decode.py` shows `0x1b[13..14]` is
**100% stable per entity and never `0x0000`** for a mobile NPC, and
that the raw `0x1b` arrives **before** the reliable `0x28` (AUGUSTO
0x0124: `0x1b` @277.953 s vs `0x28` @278.433 s) — so the client
creates the world actor from this id and `0x0000` (the old value)
meant the NPC was never instantiated. The retail values
(29002/29046/1739/…) are server runtime handles absent from every
client def (no static table exists; a fresh capture would not yield
one). Fixed by emitting the NPC's real npc.def type id (non-zero,
stable, real client data) via `NPC.getEntityClassId()`; full detail
in `udp_s2c_1b.md` "Task #178" section. It is still a different id
space from this packet's `[11..12]` class_id — that observation was
correct; what was wrong was concluding it could not be populated.

### Trailing string is the AI **script name** — RESOLVED (2026-05-17)

Earlier notes called doc `[34..]` a "type_name". Decoding a fresh
retail **live** capture (`strace/RETAIL_LIVE_p1p3_sit_npc_20260517.pcap`,
server `157.90.195.74`, player sat next to NPCs in p1/p3) with
`tools/pcap-decode.py` resolved it definitively. Three clean
`0x03/0x28` records:

| entity | doc[11..12] class | doc[34..] tokens |
|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| 266    | `0x0b4a`          | `WSK\0 -178\0 /\0` |
| 299    | `0x0001`          | `WCOP\0 45\0 9\0` |
| 325    | `0x27e2`          | `PATROL_COPBOT6\0 45\0 0\0` |

The first token is the per-spawn **AI script name** the client
SCRIPTEDPLAYER ctor (`FUN_0069a580` → `FUN_0081e310`) looks up in
its script-factory map (PROTOCOL.md ~1287). It is exactly the
world-`.dat` NPC `actorName` field (`WorldDatParser.NpcEntry.actorName`;
cf. `WorldDatParserTest` — the reaktor NPC's actorName == `"STATIC"`),
**not** a type table. `WSK`/`WCOP`/`PATROL_COPBOT6` are
canonical NC2 script names. The second token is the ASCII
orientation. Scripted NPCs (copbots/walkers) append a third
NUL-terminated token (`/`, `9`, `0`) — a small per-NPC runtime
index in the same unreproducible category as the `[19..]` state
block; the byte-pinned AUGUSTO "PMAN" reference (entity 0x0124,
2 tokens `PMAN\0 1\0`) renders in retail without it, so Ceres-J
emits the 2-token form.

**Task #178 fix:** `WorldNPCInfo.writeBody` previously wrote
`npc.getName()` (display name), and the bulk `world_npcs` bridge in
`NpcSpawnManager` passed `script_name=""`. Scripted NPCs therefore
got an empty/wrong script token → client "Unable to find script" →
NPC never instantiated. Now `NpcSpawnManager` sources `script_name`
from `world_npcs.actor_name` (the authoritative world-.dat actorName)
and `WorldNPCInfo` writes the resolved script name (curated AI script
or bridged actor_name), falling back to the display name only when no
script is set.

### Task #178c — state block is 17 B, not 15 (Type-15 corruption fixed)

The #178 tables above used the doc's hand-transcribed AUGUSTO "PMAN"
(a *non-scripted* NPC) as the layout ground truth: 15-byte state
block at doc[19..33], strings at doc[34], and a putative "3rd
trailing token". **Re-parsing the live pcap through the actual
reliable framing** (`[0x13][ctr][ack]` then `[subLen LE2][0x03][seq
LE2][body]`, per `PacketBuilderUDP1303`) shows that interpretation
was wrong for the scripted-city-NPC class — the very class the
client treats as **Type 15** and was rejecting:

```
reliable seq=31 0x28 body(45B):
  2800010a0100 0010e3ed78 4a0b a078 107f 708a
  00bc030000000c0b0709090500000000 00      <- 17-byte state [19..35]
  57534b00 2d31373800                       <- "WSK\0" "-178\0"  (body ends here)
reliable seq=33 0x28 body(54B): ...PATROL_COPBOT6\0 45\0   (body ends here)
```

The reliable sub-packet **ends exactly after the orientation token**
— there is **no 3rd token**. The earlier "3rd token" (`/`, `9`, `0`)
was a flat byte-scan bleeding into the *next* sub-packet's
`[subLen][03][seq]` header. The two bytes the PMAN model attributed
to "15 state + extra token" actually belong to the **state block**,
which is **17 bytes** (doc[19..35]) for all three live scripted
samples (266/299/325), with the script name at doc[36].

Emitting 15 (pre-#178c) put `script_name` at doc[34] — 2 bytes early
— so the client's Type-15 parser mis-framed the record and logged
`@WWORLDMGR : Corrupted Message Type:15, Size:21` with truncated
names (`'ader_pa'`); `SCRIPTEDPLAYER` never spawned the NPC. Fixed in
`WorldNPCInfo.writeBody`: 17 zero state bytes (retail-valid no-state
instance; trailing 6 bytes zero in every sample, leading bytes
unreproducible runtime state). PROTOCOL.md ~1266 carries the
corrected doc-relative table.

### Net assessment

The `0x28` packet is byte-identical to retail on every reproducible
field (17-byte state block; script name at doc[36]), and the trailing
token carries the correct per-NPC AI script name (the root #178
client-side spawn-fail trigger). Residual dependence on the `0x1b`
`entity_class_id` gap above is tracked separately and is not a
`0x28` protocol bug.

## Server-side handler

Ceres-J emits 0x03/0x28 via `server.gameserver.packets.server_udp.WorldInfoSrv`
during world-entry burst (in `WorldEntryEvent`) — currently emits
the empty form (no NPCs) since the test fixture has no zone-
resident NPCs. To match retail, need NPC roster + ASCII type name
table.

NPC spawning logic in `server.gameserver.npc.NpcSpawnManager`
loads NPCs from SQLite per zone but the test fixture doesn't
populate them. See task #129 / #167 for related NPC packet work.
